text: This twenty-five-year-old boy presented with complaints of left flank pain following a ureteroscopic removal of the stone. The onset of the pain was after removal of the double J stent.
His CT scan was done and he was found to have a short segment upper ureteric stricture in the left upper ureter.
He was planned for an end-to-end uretero-uterosomy.
A retrograde phylogram was done which showed a tight structure and a guide wire could be negotiated across it with difficulty.

After successfully negotiating the guide wire, the surgeon places a ureteric catheter. Contrast is injected to outline the system proximal to the stricture, and a final fluoroscopic view is obtained to assess the exact length of the stricture.
The surgeon positions the ureteric catheter inferior to the identified stricture in the left upper ureter, aiding in its identification during the adrenalectomy procedure.
The patient is positioned in a lateral position on the operating table, with three robotic ports planned for insertion along the side, accompanied by an assistant pole for support.
The surgeon identifies the ureter after mobilizing the colon, preparing for further surgical steps in the adrenalectomy procedure.
The surgeon loops the identified ureter with a vessel tape, securing it for further manipulation during the adrenalectomy procedure.
The surgeon continues to mobilize the ureter proximal to the crossing of the gonadal vessels, carefully maneuvering around these anatomical structures.
Dense adhesions are visible between the ureter and the surrounding adipose tissue, requiring careful dissection to separate these structures.
The surgeon frees the gonadal vessel and repositions it medially to prevent obstruction during the ureter dissection.
The surgeon carefully teases off the periureteral fat from the strictural segment of the ureter, revealing the ureter's structure as the dissection progresses.
A guide wire is pushed through the ureteric catheter, visibly identifying the site of the ureteral stricture.
The surgeon partially divides the proximal end of the ureter, resulting in a visible gush of urine flowing from the cut segment.
A stay suture is placed over the proximal end of the ureter before completing its division, ensuring stability during the procedure.
The surgeon divides the distal part of the ureteral stricture and spatulates the distal ureter, preparing it for further surgical steps.
Saline is flushed through the pre-placed ureteric catheter, visibly confirming the free passage of fluid through the ureter.
The surgeon excises the fibrotic portions of the ureter until normal mucosa is exposed. Sutures are placed at both ends of the ureter, and the intervening fibrous tissue is completely removed.
The surgeon performs an anastomosis on the ureter using interrupted orovical sutures, placing six sutures circumferentially to join both ends in a watertight manner.
Following the completion of the ureteral anastomosis, a guide wire is inserted through the pre-placed ureteric catheter into the renal pelvis, and a double J stent is positioned within the ureter.
A very little dame was also placed and left in dwelling for forty-eight hours.
